what_it_does: Documents the exact per-engine installation steps, runtime environment variables, shared HTTP contract, port assignments, and current verification status for five isolated text-to-speech engine services (chatterbox, orpheus, cosyvoice, xtts, qwen3tts), plus the local speech-to-text/ears setup and the voice loop.
observations: Each engine runs as its own HTTP service on its own port from its own venv because dependency pins conflict.
The shared HTTP contract mirrors voice/tts_service.py: POST /tts, GET /voices, GET /health, and loud 500 errors.
COMPANY_VOICE_REF is required by chatterbox, xtts, and cosyvoice, but the refined-australian.wav clip does not exist yet.
The speed parameter is applied only by xtts; the other engines accept it for contract conformance but do not apply it.
chatterbox, orpheus, qwen3tts, and cosyvoice are under permissive licenses; xtts is under the non-commercial Coqui Public Model License.
CosyVoice installation is blocked: openai-whisper==20231117 fails to build under modern build isolation, and the wrapper cannot yet import AutoModel.
xtts latest (0.27.5) is broken; the documented working import combination is coqui-tts==0.25.3 with transformers<4.50.
qwen3tts model is already present in the local HuggingFace cache.
Local ears (faster-whisper + silero-vad) are installed and verified end-to-end on CPU in two separate venvs.
The loop runs in the shared .voice-venv and reaches the brain over HTTP at COMPANY_BRIDGE_URL.
Optional accelerators such as TensorRT and flash-attn are treated as a later tuning pass, not part of the first install.
standouts: Service-per-port and per-venv isolation is the chosen architectural shape for dependency conflicts.
Install blockers are documented as first-class content with exact failure context and next steps.
XTTS has a loud non-commercial license gate that excludes production use.
The required reference clip is missing, blocking three engines.
Local STT is already installed and verified end-to-end on CPU.
conventions: Living reference document with YAML-style frontmatter (type, module, aliases, tags, status).
Uses exact shell commands, environment-variable tables, and explicit verification dates.
Fail-loud error contract across all engines.
Version pinning is recorded together with upstream breakage notes.
Required install steps are separated from optional performance accelerators.
concerns: Multiple engines cannot run yet due to missing reference clip, dependency blockers, or pending GPU restart.
xtts is non-commercial, so it cannot be used in any production path.
CosyVoice requirements include outdated or heavy pins and optional deps that complicate installation.
orpheus is flagged as the heaviest VRAM user and may conflict with the chat brain on a 16 GB card.
Most engines accept but ignore the speed parameter, which could still surprise callers despite being documented.
notes: A battle-log style install reference that records exact commands, what currently breaks, and what is still missing before the isolated voice engines can actually run.
questions: Where will the refined-australian.wav reference clip come from and when will it be available?
Which engine will be selected for production given the XTTS license restriction and CosyVoice install blocker?
What is the timeline for the GPU/RAM restart, and which engines will be validated afterward?
Is the bridge already configured to proxy ports 4124-4128 in addition to the existing Kokoro service on 4123?
How will the loop handle requests where speed is accepted but not applied?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose is a per-engine exact install reference, and the file does exactly that while also recording current blockers, verification status, and the shared runtime contract.
apparent_themes: Isolation over integration: one service and venv per engine.
Documented failure and blockers rather than omitting them.
Separation of required install from optional performance tuning.
intention_signals: The file is trying to turn a messy, dependency-conflicting, GPU-pending voice-engine landscape into a reproducible, auditable installation runbook that a future operator can execute port-by-port without guessing.
novelty: It treats install blockers and broken upstream pins as first-class content, not obstacles to omit, making the requirements file also a verified-failure log with explicit next steps.
contribution: Records the per-engine install commands, environment-variable contract, port mapping, license constraints, and current blocker notes that any fusion would need to reproduce or resume the voice-engine setup.
summary_for_embedding: A living reference documenting exact installation, environment, HTTP contract, port mapping, license constraints, and current blockers for five isolated TTS engine services and the local STT stack in company/voice/engines.
intention_for_embedding: To give operators a reproducible, per-engine install and runtime checklist for bringing up isolated voice engines once hardware and reference assets are ready.
